headline: US and Iran reignite their war over the Strait of Hormuz: Trump drops a 20% shipping fee but presses a naval blockade as strikes fly, pushing oil to a one-month high.
the_big_picture: The single thread running through today is the re-escalation of the US-Iran conflict at the Strait of Hormuz, the chokepoint that carries roughly a fifth of the world's oil. Trump backed off a threatened 20% transit fee but is pushing ahead with a naval blockade while both sides trade strikes and tankers come under attack, sending crude to a one-month high. That oil spike is the hinge for everything downstream: US inflation cooled to 3.5% but officials flagged fresh upside risk, India's inflation just breached its central bank's target, the rupee slid past 96 and the Sensex fell. Running underneath, an AI storyline cuts both ways: IBM shed roughly $70bn in a day after stumbling on AI deals, even as China's AI-driven exports jumped 27% and Nvidia began shipping powerful H200 chips into China.

stories.title: Canada's record wildfires push New York and Washington to 'most polluted' — as air turns hazardous across the US Northeast and Midwest
key_points: 800+ wildfires burning across Canada this summer
Smoke pushed NYC and Washington DC into world's most-polluted cities
Washington hit 'Code Purple' — very unhealthy for everyone
Officials say Ontario fires are easing; air should improve
stories.background: Canada is having another catastrophic fire season — about 3,500 fires have burned more than 6 million acres this summer, driven by hot, dry conditions that scientists link to a warming climate. Smoke from these fires doesn't stay put: high-altitude winds carry it hundreds of miles south, blanketing US cities. This has become a near-yearly pattern, and it flared into a political row a day earlier when President Trump threatened new tariffs on Canada over the 'filthy' air.
stories.what_happened: Thick haze from more than 800 active Canadian wildfires — including a dozen recent flare-ups in Ontario — pushed New York City and Washington, DC into the ranks of the world's most polluted major cities on Saturday. Washington was placed under a 'Code Purple' alert, meaning the air was 'very unhealthy' for everyone, and parts of northeast Minnesota reached 'hazardous' levels. Air-quality alerts stretched from Minnesota to the East Coast. Canadian officials said fire activity in Ontario is beginning to ease and expect air to improve.
stories.why_it_matters: This is a public-health event, not just a hazy sky: fine wildfire particles (PM2.5) lodge deep in the lungs and bloodstream, worsening asthma, heart and lung disease — and 'very unhealthy' air tells even healthy people to stay indoors. It shows how a fire in one country becomes a health emergency for tens of millions in another, with no border to stop it.
stories.ripple_effects: Schools cancel outdoor activities, flights and outdoor events face disruption, and hospitals see more breathing and cardiac cases. Repeated smoke seasons raise costs for insurers and healthcare systems and push demand for air purifiers and filtration. It also keeps straining US–Canada relations, already frayed by Trump's tariff threat over the smoke.
stories.why_now: Peak North American fire season collides with weather patterns that funnel Ontario's smoke straight into densely populated US corridors — and this weekend's plume happened to settle over the biggest East Coast cities.
stories.watch_next: Whether the promised improvement holds as winds shift, and if any US cities issue fresh Code Purple/hazardous alerts into next week.

key_terms.definition: A 0–500+ scale that rates how polluted the air is; higher is worse. 'Code Purple' (roughly 201–300) means 'very unhealthy' for everyone.
Tiny airborne particles under 2.5 micrometres wide — the main danger in wildfire smoke, small enough to reach deep into the lungs and enter the blood.

stories.the_lesson: Pollution ignores borders: a fire season in one country can become a health emergency for tens of millions in another. As the climate warms, 'someone else's wildfire' increasingly becomes everyone's air.
stories.market_link: Repeated record fire seasons pressure Canadian and US property insurers and lift demand for air-quality and filtration products, while airlines and outdoor-event operators face weather-style disruption.

thread.previously: Yesterday we covered the political angle: Trump calling Canada's drifting wildfire smoke 'Willful Negligence' and threatening tariffs, with Canada's PM Carney saying both nations share responsibility for the climate change driving the fires.
thread.changed: Today the story is the health emergency itself — smoke pushed New York and Washington into the world's most-polluted cities, Washington hit 'Code Purple,' and parts of Minnesota reached hazardous air, though officials now say Ontario's fires are easing.

prev_ref.title: Trump threatens to make Canada pay for its wildfire smoke — through tariffs — as 'filthy' haze from record fires chokes US cities

stories.title: Ukraine's leadership is in turmoil mid-war: Zelensky sacked his star defence minister, crowds are protesting for a third day, and his top general may be next
key_points: Zelensky fired 35-year-old defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov
Thousands protested in Kyiv for a third straight day
Protesters want Fedorov back — and army chief Syrskyi gone
A rare public rift over how to fight the war
stories.background: Ukraine has been fighting Russia's full-scale invasion for nearly four and a half years. For most of that time the country projected unity, but a quiet fight has been building at the top over HOW to fight: Fedorov, a young tech-first minister, is credited with the cheap drones and unmanned systems that have let Ukraine punch above its weight, while 60-year-old Commander-in-Chief Oleksandr Syrskyi — a general trained in the Soviet era — favours classic, centralised command: more infantry, hold ground. The two clashed, and Zelensky sided with his general.
stories.what_happened: On July 15 Zelensky removed Fedorov, the popular defence minister, as part of a wider government reshuffle that also replaced the prime minister. That triggered protests: crowds gathered in Kyiv and other cities for a third consecutive day on July 18, with two demands — reinstate Fedorov and sack Syrskyi. Reporting (FT, Bloomberg) says Zelensky is now considering replacing Syrskyi too, with interviews for candidates expected over the July 18–19 weekend. Ukraine is currently fighting under an interim defence chief.
stories.why_it_matters: This is the first serious crack in Ukraine's wartime unity, and it's spilling into the streets while the war still rages. It is a live test of Zelensky's political authority: firing a popular reformer to protect an old-guard general risks looking like he's choosing loyalty over the innovation that has kept Ukraine competitive against a far larger army.
stories.ripple_effects: Turmoil at the top can slow weapons decisions and unsettle Western allies who fund and arm Ukraine — they want a stable, predictable partner. A leadership fight over drones-vs-troops could shape whether Ukraine keeps its technological edge or reverts to attrition warfare it can't win on numbers. It also hands Moscow a propaganda gift at a moment when Russia is escalating missile strikes on Ukrainian cities.
stories.why_now: The reshuffle landed just as Russia intensified its bombardment (a major ballistic-missile attack hit Kyiv this week), sharpening the debate over which strategy is working — and street anger has built with each day Fedorov stays out and Syrskyi stays in.
stories.watch_next: Whether Zelensky actually removes Syrskyi after this weekend's candidate interviews — or holds firm and rides out the protests.

key_terms.definition: The top uniformed military officer who runs battlefield operations — distinct from the defence minister, who is a civilian official handling policy, budgets and procurement.
When a leader reassigns or removes senior ministers, changing the makeup of the cabinet without a new election.

stories.the_lesson: Even a country united by an outside enemy can fracture over strategy — and in modern war, the fight between 'more technology' and 'more troops' is itself a decisive front. How an army chooses to fight can matter as much as how much it spends.
stories.market_link: Deepening political turmoil in a country at war can add risk premium to European defence stocks and to wheat and oil, both sensitive to the Russia-Ukraine conflict.

stories.title: Europe's new digital border system is tripling airport queues — a summer-travel headache as fingerprints replace passport stamps
key_points: The EU's Entry/Exit System (EES) is slowing passport control for non-EU travellers
Border times up from ~7 to ~20 minutes at Rome; 1–2 hour queues reported
Non-EU visitors must now give fingerprints and a face scan on arrival
Ryanair tells passengers to allow extra time for the 'failed rollout'
stories.background: The Schengen Area is the group of 29 European countries that have abolished passport checks between one another, so a traveller crossing into any of them from outside is checked once at the external border. To tighten that border, the EU has been rolling out the Entry/Exit System (EES) since last October — a digital system that replaces the old ink passport stamp with a biometric record: non-EU arrivals register their fingerprints and a facial scan, which are then checked when they leave, to track who overstays.
stories.what_happened: Airport bosses and airlines say the EES is sharply increasing wait times as staff enrol travellers' biometrics for the first time. At Rome's Fiumicino, average clearance for UK nationals has risen from about 7 minutes to 20, with occasional one-to-two-hour queues; Portugal's Faro and Spain's Barcelona have reported 30–60 minute waits. Ryanair has urged passengers to 'allow extra time' and blamed a 'failed EES rollout', while the European Commission says disruption at most airports is limited and it will keep supporting member states.
stories.why_it_matters: This hits millions of ordinary travellers at the peak of the European summer — Britons and other non-EU visitors face longer, less predictable journeys, missed connections and knock-on strain on airlines and airports. It is a concrete example of how a security upgrade can impose real friction and cost on everyday movement before the technology beds in.
stories.ripple_effects: Longer queues raise costs and complaints for airlines and airports and could dent tourism spending in peak season; expect pressure on the EU to speed up e-gate integration. A linked, larger change looms: a new online travel-authorisation scheme (ETIAS) is due to follow, adding another step for visitors.
stories.why_now: The pain is peaking now because the phased rollout has reached busy hubs during the height of the summer holiday travel rush, when passenger volumes are at their highest.
stories.watch_next: Whether more airports connect the EES to automated e-gates to cut queues, and the timeline for the follow-on ETIAS travel-authorisation requirement.

key_terms.definition: A bloc of 29 European countries that have scrapped passport checks between each other, so travellers are checked only at the external border.
Physical identifiers unique to a person — such as fingerprints or a facial scan — used to verify identity.
The EU's new digital border system that records non-EU travellers' biometric data on entry and exit, replacing manual passport stamps.

stories.the_lesson: New security or tracking systems almost always trade short-term friction for long-term control — the promised benefits arrive slowly, but the queues and costs land immediately.
stories.market_link: Watch European airline and airport operator stocks (e.g. Ryanair, IAG, Aena) if travel disruption drags into peak-season results.

stories.title: Cuba frees dissident artist Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara after 5 years — on condition he leave the country, and he lands in Miami
key_points: Cuban artist and 'Patria y Vida' co-writer released after a 5-year jail term
Freed on condition he go into exile; the US granted him parole
Greeted by a flag-waving crowd on arrival in Miami
Rights groups had long called him a political prisoner
stories.background: Luis Manuel Otero Alcántara, 38, is one of Cuba's best-known dissidents — a performance artist who co-founded the San Isidro Movement of Havana artists and writers, and who shared a Grammy for 'Patria y Vida' ('Homeland and Life'), a song that became an anthem for opponents of Cuba's one-party government. He was arrested during the mass anti-government protests of July 11, 2021, and in 2022 a court sentenced him to five years for 'public disorder', contempt and disrespecting national symbols. Amnesty International and others called him a political prisoner; Havana rejected the label.
stories.what_happened: Cuba released Otero Alcántara after his five-year sentence, but on the condition that he leave the country — a form of forced exile. The United States granted him parole (permission to enter), and he arrived in Miami, where a crowd cheered, sang and draped him in a Cuban flag bearing the words 'Patria y Vida'.
stories.why_it_matters: Freeing a marquee dissident only by pushing him into exile is a well-worn authoritarian tactic: it removes a symbol from the streets without conceding that he was wrongly jailed. It also hands Cuba's opposition abroad a powerful, internationally recognised voice — and reopens questions about the many activists still detained since 2021.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect renewed pressure on Havana over remaining political prisoners, and a boost to the Miami-based Cuban diaspora's advocacy. It lands amid a broader Cuban crisis of blackouts and economic collapse, and could feature in US–Cuba policy debates.
stories.why_now: His five-year term had run its course, and the exile-and-parole arrangement came together this week, with his arrival in the US over the weekend making it a live story now.
stories.watch_next: Whether Cuba releases other jailed 2021 protesters, and how Otero Alcántara uses his new platform from exile.

key_terms.definition: Someone who openly opposes an authoritarian government and is often persecuted for it.
US permission for someone to enter the country temporarily for urgent humanitarian or public-interest reasons, without a standard visa.
Releasing or expelling a person from prison or their country on the condition they leave and not return — removing a critic without admitting fault.

stories.the_lesson: Governments often manage dissent by exporting it — freeing a prominent critic into exile silences them at home while avoiding any admission that the jailing was unjust.

stories.title: Drone strikes halt a major Black Sea oil pipeline's exports — a new front in the Russia-Ukraine war threatens oil supply
key_points: Drones hit two tankers loading at the Caspian Pipeline's Black Sea terminal
CPC suspended loadings; it ships ~1.58 million barrels a day of mostly Kazakh crude
One tanker caught fire (later controlled); no spill or casualties reported
Attacks on Black Sea shipping have escalated sharply this past week
stories.background: The Caspian Pipeline Consortium (CPC) carries crude oil roughly 1,500 km from Kazakhstan's giant oilfields across southern Russia to an export terminal on Russia's Black Sea coast near Novorossiysk, where it's loaded onto tankers. It's the main export route for landlocked Kazakhstan and involves Western majors like Chevron and ExxonMobil. Russia and Ukraine have been trading escalating drone strikes, increasingly aimed at each other's ships and energy sites.
stories.what_happened: On Sunday, July 19, the CPC said oil loading at its Black Sea terminal was suspended after drone attacks on two tankers — the Asia and the Nissos Ios — while they were being loaded. The Asia caught fire, which was later controlled; the consortium reported no oil spill and no casualties, and shut moorings 1 and 3. CPC did not assign blame; a Chevron-linked tanker was among those hit.
stories.why_it_matters: The CPC handles about 1.58 million barrels a day — roughly 1.5% of world oil supply and the lifeline for Kazakhstan's exports. Any prolonged halt tightens the global oil market and can push prices up, and it drags Kazakhstan and Western oil majors into a war they are not party to.
stories.ripple_effects: Oil prices can rise on supply-disruption fears; Kazakhstan loses export revenue and may scramble for alternative routes; shippers face higher war-risk insurance in the Black Sea. Stacked on top of the Gulf tensions from the US-Iran war, it piles up global energy-supply risk that traders, refiners and central banks all watch.
stories.why_now: Russia and Ukraine have sharply escalated drone attacks on each other's shipping and energy infrastructure in the Black and Azov seas over the past week, and the CPC terminal sits within drone range.
stories.watch_next: How long loadings stay halted, whether oil prices spike, and whether attacks on Black Sea shipping keep spreading.

key_terms.definition: A pipeline carrying Kazakh crude oil across Russia to a Black Sea export terminal — one of the world's key oil-supply routes.
Pumping oil into tankers at a port terminal so it can be shipped to refineries in other countries.

stories.the_lesson: Global oil supply runs through a handful of chokepoints and terminals; damage to even one export node — far from the buyer — can ripple into prices worldwide, showing how concentrated and fragile energy logistics really are.
stories.market_link: Oil (Brent/WTI) can climb on the supply threat; watch Chevron, ExxonMobil, and tanker/shipping and marine-insurance stocks.

stories.title: Iran hits Kuwait's water-and-power plants, forcing rationing — the Gulf war spreads to civilian lifelines
key_points: Iran struck a second Kuwaiti power-and-desalination plant in 24 hours
Fire forced generating units offline; Kuwait told people to ration electricity
About 90% of Kuwait's drinking water comes from desalination
War with the US now hits Gulf civilians' basic services, not just bases
stories.background: The US and Iran have been in an escalating shooting war for over a week — the US bombing near Tehran, Iranian missiles killing US troops in Jordan. Kuwait is a small, oil-rich US ally on the Persian Gulf that hosts American forces, so Iran has been firing at US bases there and now at infrastructure. Crucially, Kuwait has almost no natural fresh water: it turns seawater into drinking water at big coastal desalination plants that also generate much of its electricity.
stories.what_happened: Early Saturday, July 18, a fire broke out at a second Kuwaiti power-generation and water-desalination plant after an Iranian strike — the second such facility hit within 24 hours. Kuwait's energy ministry took several generating units offline 'to preserve the safety of the facility and the stability of the grid' and urged residents to ration power at peak hours. Iran's Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) said it targeted US troop-deployment sites, launch pads and HIMARS rocket systems in Kuwait; Kuwait said the plants themselves were damaged. No casualties were reported, and by July 19 one plant was still burning.
stories.why_it_matters: These plants are Kuwait's lifeline: roughly 90% of its drinking water is desalinated seawater, and the same plants make much of its power — in summer heat that tops 45°C. Hitting them threatens water and electricity for ordinary civilians, not just soldiers, and marks a dangerous widening of the war onto the Gulf states that host US bases.
stories.ripple_effects: Other Gulf states that lean heavily on desalination and host US forces — Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Qatar, Bahrain — now look exposed too. Insurers, shippers and oil traders price in more Gulf risk, nudging up oil prices and marine-insurance costs. Kuwait may need emergency power and water measures, and each strike raises the odds the conflict pulls in more countries.
stories.why_now: Iran is retaliating for a week of intensifying US strikes inside its territory and trying to raise the cost to Washington's Gulf allies for hosting American troops — shifting from military bases toward the infrastructure that keeps those allies running.
stories.watch_next: Whether Iran strikes a third plant or a Saudi/UAE facility, and how quickly Kuwait restores full water and power.

key_terms.definition: Turning salty seawater into fresh drinking water by removing the salt, usually at large coastal plants that often also generate electricity.
Iran's elite military force, separate from its regular army, that runs many of its missile and drone operations.

stories.the_lesson: In modern wars the most damaging targets aren't always armies — hitting the plants that supply a country's water and power can hurt a whole population fast, so heavy dependence on a few critical facilities is itself a strategic weakness.
stories.market_link: Rising Gulf conflict risk tends to lift oil prices and tanker/marine-insurance costs; Gulf equity indices sold off as hostilities intensified.

thread.previously: We reported the US had widened its air war — bombing near Tehran for the first time and disabling an Iranian tanker — as Iran fired back at US bases across the Gulf.
thread.changed: Iran's retaliation has now moved from US bases to civilian lifelines: it has hit two Kuwaiti water-desalination and power plants within 24 hours, forcing Kuwait to ration electricity.

prev_ref.title: US widens its war on Iran — bombing near Tehran for the first time and disabling a tanker, as Iran fires back at American bases in the Gulf

stories.title: Britain's Josh Kerr breaks a 27-year-old mile world record — the first man ever under 3:43
key_points: Ran 3:42.66, first man to break the 3:43 barrier
Broke Hicham El Guerrouj's 1999 record by nearly half a second
Set at the Diamond League meet in London
Planned the record bid months ahead as 'Project 222'
stories.background: The mile is track's most storied distance — the event of Roger Bannister's first sub-4-minute run in 1954. Its world record had stood untouched since 1999, when Morocco's Hicham El Guerrouj ran 3 minutes 43.13 seconds, a mark so durable that many thought it might last a generation. The 'Diamond League' is the sport's top annual circuit of one-day meets, where the best runners chase fast times and prize money.
stories.what_happened: On Saturday, July 18, 2026, at the Diamond League meet at London Stadium, British runner Josh Kerr ran 3:42.66 to break El Guerrouj's 27-year-old record by nearly half a second, becoming the first man ever to go under 3:43. Kerr had announced the attempt months earlier, dubbing it 'Project 222' (the number of seconds — 3:42 — he needed to beat). Two training partners set the pace, and he finished about three seconds clear of American Yared Nuguse.
stories.why_it_matters: Breaking one of athletics' most iconic and long-standing records is a landmark moment for the sport — the kind of result that headlines a season with no Olympics or World Championships. It also shows how a meticulously planned, pace-setter-assisted attempt can crack a barrier that stood for a generation.
stories.ripple_effects: The run raises Kerr's profile ahead of future championships and reignites his rivalry with the world's other middle-distance stars, likely drawing bigger crowds and sponsor interest to the mile. It also nudges rivals to chase the new mark, and revives debate about how much modern shoe technology and choreographed pacing help rewrite the record books.
stories.why_now: It surfaced this weekend because the record was set Saturday night in London; a feat announced in advance and then delivered on schedule made it an even bigger story.
stories.watch_next: Whether Kerr or a rival lowers the mark again this season, and how he performs when he next races the sport's other elite milers head-to-head.

key_terms.definition: Athletics' premier annual series of one-day international meetings, where top track-and-field athletes compete for points and prize money.
A runner whose job is to set a fast, even speed for part of a race to help a star athlete chase a record, then usually drop out.

stories.the_lesson: Even 'unbreakable' records fall to a clear plan executed patiently — naming the target, engineering the conditions, and building toward it beats simply hoping for a great day.

stories.title: A passenger ferry carrying 116 people capsizes off Guyana — a frantic overnight rescue pulls dozens from the water
key_points: The MV Barima capsized on a Georgetown-to-Port Kaituma run
116 people aboard; more than 50 rescued so far
Distress call came around 11pm local time overnight
The vessel was built in 1939 — 87 years old
stories.background: Guyana is a small country on the northern coast of South America. Large parts of its interior have no roads, so government-run river-and-coastal ferries are a lifeline that ordinary people rely on to reach remote towns like Port Kaituma in the northwest. When one of those boats fails, there is often no quick backup, and help can be hours away by water.
stories.what_happened: Overnight into Sunday, July 19, 2026, the MV Barima — a ferry with 116 people aboard, travelling from the capital Georgetown toward Port Kaituma — capsized near Iron Punt off Guyana's coast. A distress call came in around 11:01pm local time, triggering a scramble of state and private boats. Prime Minister Mark Phillips said at least 53 people had been rescued; officials said the search for the rest was still going. The boat, built in 1939, was carrying 250 life jackets and several life rafts.
stories.why_it_matters: This is a mass-casualty emergency still unfolding in real time — dozens of people are unaccounted for, and their fate depends on how fast rescuers can reach them in open water. It also puts a spotlight on the age and safety of the boats a whole region depends on to simply get around.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect scrutiny of why an 87-year-old vessel was carrying so many passengers, and likely government promises to review ferry safety and overcrowding rules. For the isolated communities the route serves, any suspension of the service cuts off their main link to the outside world. The final death toll — still unknown — will shape how big a national reckoning this becomes.
stories.why_now: The story is surfacing today because the accident happened just hours ago and the rescue is live; the numbers (how many saved, how many lost) are still changing as boats return to shore.
stories.watch_next: The confirmed toll once the search ends, and any early word on what caused the capsize — overloading, weather, or a fault in the aging vessel.

key_terms.definition: When a boat tips over or turns upside down in the water, throwing those aboard into the sea.

stories.the_lesson: When infrastructure is old and there is no backup, a single failure becomes a disaster — the safety of everyday transport depends as much on maintenance and honest capacity limits as on rescue crews arriving fast.

stories.title: Tate brothers arrested in Miami as UK seeks their extradition on 59 charges including rape and trafficking
key_points: US Marshals arrest Andrew and Tristan Tate in Miami on a sealed warrant
UK announces 59 charges tied to 7 alleged victims (2010–2017)
Andrew faces 42 charges, Tristan 17; both deny wrongdoing
Britain is now seeking their extradition from the US
stories.background: Andrew Tate is a former kickboxer turned online personality with a huge, mostly young-male following, known for 'hyper-masculine' content that critics call misogynistic. He and his brother Tristan have faced criminal investigations in several countries for years, including a separate case in Romania. This is the first time UK charges have led to their arrest on US soil.
stories.what_happened: US Marshals arrested the brothers on Saturday in Miami on a sealed warrant after British prosecutors announced a total of 59 charges and requested their extradition. Andrew Tate faces 42 charges including rape, human trafficking, indecent images of a child and assault; Tristan faces 17 including rape, sexual assault and trafficking. The UK's Crown Prosecution Service says the offences involve seven women in the East of England between 2010 and 2017. Andrew's lawyers say the claims are false and any activity was consensual.
stories.why_it_matters: It is a major escalation in a years-long, cross-border legal saga involving one of the internet's most influential and divisive figures. It also tests whether the US will extradite high-profile residents to face UK justice, and how courts handle crimes alleged to have happened years ago.
stories.ripple_effects: The case could set the pace for a lengthy extradition fight and revive debate over online 'manosphere' influencers and their effect on young men. Platforms and advertisers may face renewed pressure over the reach of such content, and the outcome will be watched closely by the alleged victims and by rights groups.
stories.why_now: British prosecutors formally expanded the charge sheet and issued an extradition request, giving US Marshals the legal basis to make the arrest this weekend.
stories.watch_next: The US extradition hearing — whether a court agrees to send the brothers to Britain, and any bail decision in the meantime.

key_terms.definition: The formal process by which one country hands over a suspect to another country to face criminal charges there.
An arrest order kept secret from the public (and often the suspect) until the arrest is carried out.
The main agency that decides on and conducts criminal prosecutions in England and Wales.

stories.the_lesson: Borders don't erase criminal liability: extradition treaties let charges filed in one country reach a suspect living in another — but proving the case and completing the transfer can take years.

stories.title: Russia hits Kyiv with one of the biggest missile barrages of the war — 40+ missiles and 120+ drones
key_points: Over 40 missiles and 120 drones, most aimed at Kyiv
At least 1 killed and 16 wounded in the capital
Includes 10 Zircon hypersonic missiles that are hard to shoot down
Ukraine says it downed 17 ballistic missiles, hinting at fresh Patriot stocks
stories.background: Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine has ground on for more than four years, and in recent weeks both sides have escalated long-range strikes on each other's cities and infrastructure. Just yesterday Ukraine launched its deadliest strike inside Russia in over two years, torching warehouses and an oil depot; this barrage is Russia's answer.
stories.what_happened: Overnight into Sunday, Russia fired more than 40 missiles of various types and about 120 attack drones, with most aimed at Kyiv, President Zelensky said. Strikes hit five districts of the capital, killing at least one person and wounding 16; across Ukraine at least 16 civilians were killed in 24 hours. Ukraine says it shot down 17 of the ballistic missiles and 108 drones.
stories.why_it_matters: This is one of the largest single attacks on Kyiv since the war began, and it shows the air war is intensifying rather than winding down. The use of Zircon hypersonic missiles — which fly many times the speed of sound and are very hard to intercept — tests the limits of Ukraine's Western-supplied air defences.
stories.ripple_effects: Heavy attacks on Ukrainian cities push Kyiv to beg allies for more interceptor missiles, straining already-tight Western stockpiles. Sustained strikes damage power and housing, drive more refugees toward Europe, and keep upward pressure on European gas and wheat prices tied to the war. They also harden the diplomatic stalemate, making any near-term ceasefire less likely.
stories.why_now: The barrage looks like direct retaliation for Ukraine's large strike inside Russia a day earlier — a tit-for-tat escalation as each side tries to show it can still hit hard deep in the other's territory.
stories.watch_next: Whether Ukraine's Western partners rush more Patriot interceptor missiles to Kyiv, and whether Russia sustains this scale of attack in the coming nights.

key_terms.definition: A missile that arcs high into or above the atmosphere and falls fast onto its target, making it hard to shoot down.
A weapon that flies at more than five times the speed of sound and can manoeuvre, giving defenders very little time to react.
A US-made air-defence missile that Ukraine uses to shoot down incoming Russian missiles.

stories.the_lesson: In modern warfare, defence is a numbers game: attackers try to overwhelm air defences with mass salvos, so a country's safety depends as much on how many interceptors it can keep in stock as on how good each one is.
stories.market_link: Prolonged strikes support European natural-gas and wheat prices and can lift defence-contractor shares (interceptor and drone makers), while adding a risk premium to oil.

thread.previously: Yesterday we covered Ukraine's deadliest strike inside Russia in over two years, hitting a Wildberries logistics hub and an oil depot and killing at least 8.
thread.changed: Now Russia has retaliated with one of its biggest missile-and-drone barrages of the war against Kyiv, killing at least one and wounding 16 — the exchange is escalating, not cooling.
